questions.question: Explain the Bell‑LaPadula model and its two primary security properties.
keyPoints: Simple Security Property – no read up
Uses security clearance levels (e.g., Top Secret, Secret)

questions.question: Differentiate between Mandatory Access Control (MAC) and Role‑Based Access Control (RBAC) in terms of enforcement and flexibility.
keyPoints: MAC is system‑enforced based on fixed security labels
RBAC is administered through user‑assigned roles
MAC offers less flexibility, suitable for high‑security environments
RBAC provides easier permission management and scalability

questions.question: Describe the three major Access Control models—MAC, RBAC, and Attribute‑Based Access Control (ABAC)—highlighting their core principles, typical use cases, and advantages/disadvantages.
keyPoints: MAC: mandatory, label‑based, strict confidentiality, low flexibility
RBAC: role‑driven, least‑privilege, easy administration, limited to static roles
ABAC: policy‑driven using user, resource, and environment attributes, highly dynamic
Comparison: MAC excels in high‑security (military) but lacks scalability; RBAC balances simplicity and flexibility; ABAC offers fine‑grained control at the cost of complexity

questions.question: Given a multi‑department organization handling both classified and routine business data, propose an integrated access‑control solution that combines MAC, RBAC, and ABAC to meet confidentiality and operational requirements. Justify your design.
keyPoints: Apply MAC to protect classified information (e.g., defence records) using security labels
Implement RBAC for routine departmental functions (e.g., HR, Finance) to assign permissions by job title
Use ABAC for dynamic contextual decisions (e.g., time, location, device) to grant temporary access
Layer the models: MAC at the core for data classification, RBAC for day‑to‑day role assignments, ABAC on top for situational fine‑grained control, ensuring both security and operational flexibility
